criteria: The agent has read Section 4.1 (Prior Perturbation Generation) and Algorithm 1 Stage 1 to understand how an adversarial image is generated by injecting harmful features from a corpus using PGD optimization.

The agent has read Section 4.2 (Bimodal Adversarial Optimization Loop) and Algorithm 1 Stage 2 to understand the alternating greedy optimization of adversarial text suffixes and adversarial image perturbations.

The agent has read Section 5.1 (Experimental Setup) completely, including the datasets (AdvBench 520 prompts, harmful content corpus), models (MiniGPT-4, InstructBLIP, LLaVA as white-box; Gemini, GPT-4, Qwen-VL as black-box), baselines (Arondight, GCG, Advimage, ImgJP, UMK, InPieces, BAP, MLAI, FigStep, HADES), metrics (ASR via HarmBench with GPT-3.5-turbo; Toxicity Rate via Perspective API and Detoxify), and implementation details.
The agent has read Table 1 to understand the main ASR comparison of PBI-Attack versus ten baseline methods across all six target models.
The agent has read Appendix B to understand the ablation studies on number of queries, perturbation constraints, λ values, K values, and interaction steps.

The plan includes the two-stage PBI-Attack pipeline: Stage 1 generating a prior adversarial image from a harmful corpus, and Stage 2 performing bimodal interactive optimization by alternating adversarial text and image updates.
The plan includes the Stage 1 loss function combining negative toxicity and cross-modal feature alignment: L(x_adv) = sum_i [-T(x_adv, y_i) + lambda * ||h(x_adv) - g(y_i)||].
The plan includes the PGD-based iterative update rule for the adversarial image perturbation x_adv^p in Stage 1, using the gradient of the loss with respect to h(x_adv^p).

The plan includes the greedy adversarial text suffix selection from a predetermined suffix corpus Y^s by maximizing the toxicity score T(x_adv, y_adv || y), and concatenating the best suffix to y_adv.

The plan includes generating K random image perturbation candidates satisfying the infinity-norm bound B, greedily selecting the best candidate x_new^p that maximizes toxicity, and superimposing it onto the current adversarial image.

The plan includes integration of a toxicity evaluation model (Perspective API with 8 attributes or Detoxify with 6 attributes) as the optimization signal T(x_adv, y_adv), with multiple queries per input pair to reduce output randomness.
The plan includes loading white-box LVLMs (MiniGPT-4, InstructBLIP, LLaVA) as surrogate models, and evaluating transferability on black-box models (Gemini, GPT-4, Qwen-VL).
Image feature extraction function h(·) and text feature extraction function g(·) are implemented using a white-box LVLM (e.g., MiniGPT-4 or BLIP), enabling cross-modal feature alignment.

The image superimposition operation x_adv = x_benign ⊕ x_adv^p is implemented via the feature extraction function h(·), combining the benign image and adversarial perturbation in feature space as defined in Equation 1.
The Stage 1 loss function L(x_adv) = sum_{i=1}^{m} [-T(x_adv, y_i) + lambda * ||h(x_adv) - g(y_i)||] is implemented, correctly combining negated toxicity scores and L2 cross-modal feature alignment.
The PGD update rule x_adv^p = h^{-1}(h(x_adv^p) - eta * grad_{h(x_adv^p)} L(x_adv)) is implemented for iteratively updating the adversarial image perturbation in Stage 1.
The Stage 1 optimization loop is implemented to run PGD updates until L(x_adv) converges, ensuring harmful features from the corpus Y are fully embedded into the adversarial image.

The toxicity scoring function T(x_adv, y_adv) is implemented using Perspective API (8 attributes: toxicity, severe toxicity, identity attack, insult, profanity, threat, sexually explicit, flirtation) or Detoxify classifier (6 attributes), aggregating scores across all attributes.
The toxicity evaluation queries the target model 10 times per adversarial input pair and uses the mean toxicity score across all responses to reduce the high randomness of LVLM outputs.

The greedy adversarial text optimization is implemented: selecting the best suffix y_new^s = argmax_{y in Y^s} T(x_adv, y_adv || y) from the corpus Y^s, then updating y_adv = y_adv || y_new^s (Equations 4 and 5).
K random image perturbation candidates X^p = {x_j^p}_{j=1}^K are generated satisfying the infinity-norm perturbation constraint ||h(x_j^p)||_inf <= B for the adversarial image optimization step.
The greedy adversarial image optimization is implemented: selecting x_new^p = argmax_{x in X^p} T(x_adv ⊕ x, y_adv) and updating x_adv = x_adv ⊕ x_new^p (Equations 6 and 7).

The complete bimodal optimization loop (Algorithm 1 Stage 2) is implemented: alternating between adversarial text and image optimization updates, terminating when toxicity T(x_adv, y_adv) >= T_toxicity (success) or iteration count t reaches N (failure).
White-box LVLM models MiniGPT-4 (Vicuna-13B), InstructBLIP (Vicuna-13B), and LLaVA (LLaMA-2-13B) are loaded and configured as surrogate models for feature extraction and optimization.
The AdvBench dataset (520 harmful prompts) is loaded as the test set, and a harmful content corpus is loaded for Stage 1 prior perturbation generation.
HarmBench with GPT-3.5-turbo is integrated to compute Attack Success Rate (ASR) as the proportion of prompts that result in successful jailbreaks.

Implementation hyperparameters are correctly set: step size alpha=1, batch size b=8, lambda=1.0 in the Stage 1 loss, adversarial text suffix length=10 tokens, number of text suffix candidates |Y^s|=400, K=50 image perturbation candidates, 400 iterations for image optimization, 100 for text optimization.
Stage 1 (prior perturbation generation) runs successfully on the AdvBench harmful corpus with at least one white-box surrogate model, producing a converged adversarial image x_adv.

Stage 2 (bimodal adversarial optimization) runs successfully, alternating between adversarial text suffix selection and adversarial image prefix selection, until the toxicity threshold is met or the iteration limit is reached.

The main comparison experiment (Table 1) runs successfully: PBI-Attack tested on all three white-box models (MiniGPT-4, InstructBLIP, LLaVA) with 520 AdvBench prompts, repeating each experiment three times for mean and standard deviation.
The black-box transferability experiment (Table 1) runs successfully: adversarial inputs generated with MiniGPT-4 as surrogate are evaluated on black-box models (Gemini, GPT-4, Qwen-VL).
The comparison of prior knowledge initialization versus random perturbation initialization (Table 3) runs successfully across all six target models.
The surrogate model transferability experiments using all three white-box models as surrogates (Tables 4 and 5) run successfully with both Perspective API and Detoxify evaluation.
The ablation study on number of interaction steps (Figure 10) runs successfully, varying from 1 to 9+ steps and measuring ASR on MiniGPT-4.
The defense experiments run successfully: perturbation layer defense (Table 8) and safety-centric system prompt defense (Table 9) both evaluated across all six models.

PBI-Attack achieves ASR of approximately 94.9% on MiniGPT-4, 93.2% on InstructBLIP, and 89.3% on LLaVA (Table 1), outperforming all baseline methods including the best prior method UMK (87.5% on MiniGPT-4).

PBI-Attack achieves ASR of approximately 71.7% on Gemini, 63.2% on GPT-4, and 67.1% on Qwen-VL in the black-box setting (Table 1), outperforming all black-box baselines including Arondight (56.2% on Gemini) and HADES (63.5% on Gemini).

Prior knowledge initialization substantially outperforms random perturbation initialization by approximately 20% across all tested models (Table 3): e.g., MiniGPT-4 74.4% → 94.9%, Gemini 52.1% → 71.7%.
Toxicity score guidance outperforms jailbreak probability guidance across all three white-box models (Table 2): MiniGPT-4 93.9% → 94.9%, InstructBLIP 90.6% → 93.2%, LLaVA 82.5% → 89.3%.
Full Stage 1 & 2 (PBI-Attack) substantially outperforms Stage 1 only across tested models (Table 11): MiniGPT-4 78.2% → 94.7%, InstructBLIP 77.1% → 93.6%, Qwen-VL 53.8% → 67.4%.
The optimal lambda=1.0 achieves the highest ASR in Stage 1 loss (Figure 8): MiniGPT-4 ~95.0%, InstructBLIP ~89.0%, LLaVA ~92.0%, with performance degrading at higher or lower lambda values.
The optimal K=50 image perturbation candidates achieves the best trade-off between ASR and computation in Stage 2 (Figure 9), with no significant improvement observed beyond K=50.
The bimodal optimization ASR converges after approximately 9 interaction steps (Figure 10), reaching ~94.9% on MiniGPT-4 with no further improvement at additional steps.

Under perturbation layer defense, PBI-Attack achieves substantially higher ASR than all baselines across all models (Table 8): MiniGPT-4 75.6%, InstructBLIP 72.8%, LLaVA 64.6%, Gemini 56.7%, GPT-4 44.9%, Qwen-VL 48.0%.

Under safety-centric system prompt defense, PBI-Attack achieves substantially higher ASR than all baselines across all models (Table 9): MiniGPT-4 45.8%, InstructBLIP 43.6%, LLaVA 34.2%, Gemini 27.6%, GPT-4 18.4%, Qwen-VL 24.1%.
